For Investor A shares: Total 0.55%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses 0.54%. Institutional and Investor A shares have contractual waivers with an end date of 06/30/2025 terminable upon 90 days' notice. For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund’s most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to blackrock.com for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 4% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.29%; for Investor A shares: 0.54%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 2.21% (Institutional shares) and 2.15% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • Global rates was the main driver of the fund’s underperformance relative to its benchmark.
  • During the quarter, the fund had a neutral duration (interest rate sensitivity) position amid elevated uncertainty. Given the economic unease about an escalating global trade war, we reduced credit risk exposure and broadly lowered overall risk. The fund had modest overweight allocations to mortgages, Treasury inflation-protected securities (TIPS), and the consumer credit sector.
Contributors Detractors
Security selection within credit contributed positively to performance, particularly due to the fund’s overweight positions in the banking and technology sectors. Global rates detracted from returns, mainly due to short U.S. dollar rates positioning in the global cross-market rates swap strategy. The Tactical Asset Allocation (TAA) strategy weighed on performance due to incorrect demand signals, while it struggled with reversals from tariff-induced swings.

Further insight

Given the escalation in trade war rhetoric and the absence of a Donald Trump “put”, we have less conviction in credit tilts and have proactively reduced exposures to high yield and investment grade corporates. We expect prevailing macroeconomic uncertainty to dominate market themes over the rest of the first half of this year, dampening risk appetite, and have adjusted risk accordingly. We maintain small overweight allocations to mortgages and TIPS, along with an overweight exposure to the consumer credit sector. In addition, we have leaned more into the TAA strategy. The strategy remains long on duration and has turned short on credit, neutralizing the overall duration times spread (a method of measuring credit volatility in corporate bonds) exposure.

Investment approach

The fund invests under normal circumstances, at least 80% of its assets in obligations issued or guaranteed by the U.S. government, including mortgage-backed securities, mortgage pass-through securities, commercial mortgage-backed securities, debt obligations of U.S. issuers, municipal securities, asset-backed securities; and U.S. registered dollar-denominated debt obligations of foreign issuers. The fund may invest up to 10% of its assets in securities rated below investment grade(“high yield” or “junk” bonds.)

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. Fixed income risks include interest-rate and credit risk. Typically, when interest rates rise, there is a corresponding decline in bond values. Credit risk refers to the possibility that the bond issuer will not be able to make principal and interest payments. Principal of mortgage- or asset-backed securities normally may be prepaid at any time, reducing the yield and market value of those securities. Obligations of U.S. govt agencies are supported by varying degrees of credit but generally are not backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. govt. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. International investing involves special risks including, but not limited to political risks, currency fluctuations, illiquidity and volatility. These risks may be heightened for investments in emerging markets.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

[1] The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index comprises the total investment-grade bond market.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. data.title: Comparing Bitcoin Covered Call: YBTC Vs. YBIT
data.description:
A previous article on YieldMax Bitcoin Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:YBIT), a similar option-based income ETF, will be a good starting point for the Roundhill Bitcoin Covered Call Strategy ETF (YBTC). Since I had found YBIT a buy, benchmarking YBTC against YBIT should be sufficient to establish its suitability as an income plan. Unfortunately, neither YBTC nor YBIT disclose how they strategize strike selections, option repositioning frequency (rolling up or down when the theta is insignificant) or volatility harvesting. Hence, we will have to go with performance metrics as an indicator of what to expect.

Apples to Apples

Some income ETFs pay out less aggressively and keep exposure on the underlying lives for a longer tenure. In those cases, performance narratives could be very different. YBTC belongs somewhat to that category. Its payout yield of 42%, is well below the 85% payout seen for YBIT. What this means is that YBIT de-risks investors from the bitcoin underlying faster than YBTC. Whether that is good or bad depends on the investor's use case. Investors who want to deploy income elsewhere will like faster payouts. Investors who want greater exposure to bitcoin may prefer slower payouts.

When I mentioned YBTC belonging "somewhat" to the less aggressive category, I was emphasizing on the fact that while its payout is indeed less than YBIT, it is still quite high (I would consider 10-20% payout yields as significantly conservative). It could be that YBTC strikes the sweet spot of payout vs growth, which we will see in our performance analysis. The yields data from Seeking Alpha for income plans often suffers from recency biases. Looking at the overall payouts from May 2024 to date, I see YBTC has paid out 40% of capital invested, while YBIT has paid out 50%. Still higher for YBIT, but not significantly so.

Overall Performance

Comparing performance across the common period of existence, an investment of $10K would have grown by 32% if invested in YBTC vs 11% in YBIT. This can be attributed to the bitcoin rally in the latter half of the period of analysis. Since YTC has greater exposure to bitcoin, it will do better in such markets.

Therefore, let us compare performance in a downtrend. I do not see a prolonged downturn period, which would have been better to gauge sustained impacts, but we will make do with what we have. For a very short period of down move in bitcoin between 20th May 2024 to July 5th 2024, we do see YBIT holding up better than YBTC (down by 12% and 16.5% respectively).

Another interesting period, between May 2024 to October 2024, paints a compelling story. In a period where bitcoin hardly moved close to close, YBTC tracked bitcoin better, slightly outperforming in down moves and underperforming in up moves to end up with a marginally better performance. YBIT, on the other hand, does much better in down markets, but suffers on up moves (due to larger payouts and lower exposure to bitcoin) and ends up far lower than either bitcoin or YBTC in a close to close rangebound market with volatile movements in the interim. I am attributing the performance differences mostly to the payout vs exposure to bitcoin factor. It could also very well be that YBTC's strategy is indeed superior. Regardless, the fact is that YBIT does better in downtrends, while YBTC outperforms in sideways to upward trending market cycles.

What to do with YBIT and YTC

When I had given a buy rating to YBIT, it was primarily a vote for owning a significantly less volatile investment than bitcoin. Opinions may vary here, but those investors who believe bitcoin rides are scarier and need a less volatile income paying alternative will see utility in the buy call. Of course, the overall performance cuts because of the less volatile holding should balance out the rewards and YBIT's underperformance looked reasonable. Another income ETF or a perceivably lower volatile asset like Google will have to create a far better performance track record to be considered a buy.

All things considered, here is the overall takeaway on a case by case basis

1. Heavily focused on income. Looking for a more market-agnostic income ride. More bearish on bitcoin (or need more downside comfort), but not totally a non-believer. Need quicker de-risking from the underlying - Buy YBIT

2. Believer in bitcoin. Whether you seek income or not - Buy bitcoin. If you seek income, you can replicate payouts from your capital and still beat any income plan.

3. Non-believer in bitcoin - Stay out of bitcoin, YBIT, YBTC.

4. Focused on income. Open to riding along with bitcoin to a larger extent. Less bearish on bitcoin, but not totally a believer. Want to seek more upside exposure - Buy YBTC.

Hygiene Checks

YBTC scores above YBIT in terms of expenses. YBTC's 0.95% fee is significantly lower than the 4.76% fee for YBIT. However, the performance data analyzed takes this into account.

The premium and discount deviations to NAV show significant and material spikes, as is seen for YBIT due to bitcoin's sentiment volatilities. It is extremely important for investors to ensure, they spread their entries over a period of time to average out such discrepancies (rather than a bulk investment in one go).

In terms of tax efficiencies, YBTC offers a dual advantage of delaying taxable payouts (lower payouts) and optimally categorizing it as return of capital which defers taxability and allows room for better growth. For taxable accounts, this is a major advantage over YBIT, where the payouts are not only higher but categorized as a mix of ordinary income and capital gains.

data.title: Newsmax Stock: Meme Hype Meets Harsh Reality
data.description:
Conservative outlet Newsmax (NYSE:NMAX) has probably left plenty of folks spinning with its meme mania over the past couple of months. Its wild IPO in late March had its stock go parabolic, surging 2,500% in just 48 hours, before collapsing roughly 90% from its peak. The stock’s now hovering near $24, a far cry from its eye-watering all-time high of $265. Meanwhile, on the financial end, its bottom line is firmly in the red, as it blows through its cash reserves at a worrying pace. On top of that, the defamation trial with Dominion Voting Systems is an even tougher pill for investors, threatening to drag Newsmax into an even deeper hole. Also, despite the absence of any major catalysts, NMAX stock commands a valuation that's completely detached from its underlying fundamentals. Throw in a gloomy options market to the mix, and it seems virtually impossible to hit the brakes on the stock’s ongoing free-fall. Hence, Newsmax is a meme stock, among others, that’s best avoided at this point.

Conservative Clout Meets Meme Stock Chaos

Newsmax is a popular right-leaning media outfit run by Christopher Ruddy, a longtime buddy of President Donald Trump. The company pitches itself as Fox (FOX) News’s competition, going toe-to-toe in the 24-hour conservative coverage game. However, like Fox, Newsmax has evolved into a multi-platform media business, with roughly 30% of its sales from its Digital division. Though its Broadcasting division is still doing the heavy lifting, its digital and eCommerce endeavors now offer a healthier balance. Additionally, though it has done a lot in terms of diversification, its digital division is still running at a massive EBITDA loss. Streaming may save the day, but with a modest subscriber base, it's exposed to cord-cutting trends that aren’t stopping anytime soon.

Moreover, despite its relatively small size (roughly 14% of Fox Corp's market share), Newsmax has done a remarkable job of positioning itself as a force to be reckoned with in the conservative news business. It's carried by some of the most prominent cable and satellite TV providers, and thanks to the Trump effect, it has grabbed more eyeballs than ever. Also, with a dual-class share structure (common in media companies), Ruddy continues to dominate proceedings, maintaining control over major decisions. Be it a merger or a major cash injection, Ruddy and other insiders keep all the drama in the inner circle.

Speaking of drama, Newsmax’s leap into the stock market was exactly that! It opted for a relatively unconventional “mini-IPO” in going public, which helped it tap a much broader base of retail investors. It priced its IPO at $75 million, based on 7.5 million shares at $10 each.

What happened next was astonishing to say the least, with its stock opening at $14, and within 48 hours, leaping past the $250 per share mark. From its issue price of $10, this represented north of a 2,000% gain in just a couple of days. Naturally, the wild surge gave off serious GameStop (GME) vibes, with political hype, social media buzz, and a tiny float fueling the fire. Even CEO Ruddy had to chime in and caution investors that the initial trading didn’t reflect the company’s long-term value.

Nonetheless, following a powerful two-day rally, reality caught up with Newsmax stock, with it nosediving 77% in a single session. In just the third day of trading, it had lost virtually the entirety of its initial gains. By mid-April, the stock sank to the $20 to $30 range, and outside a few brief dead cat bounces, it's now stuck there ever since.

Financials, Valuation, And The Fox Gap

Despite Newsmax being a much smaller player, it’s still swinging at big brother Fox Corp. However, on paper, a lot of that seems like mostly talk, with Fox nearly 80 times larger in sales and firmly in the green on the bottom line.

Though its YOY revenue surge of 26.4% outpaces Fox’s 15.7% jump, Newsmax’s apparent top-line momentum masks its worrying profitability woes. In terms of valuation, though, Newsmax trades close to six times trailing sales, compared to Fox at just 1.5x. Similarly, its enterprise-value-to-sales ratio at 19.2× dwarfs Fox’s ratio at around 1.6×. Meanwhile, earnings multiples appear nonexistent, but Fox trades mostly in line with its peers at roughly 13 times earnings. To put things in perspective, Newsmax at its peak was in the same vicinity as established media giants in Paramount Global (PARA) and The New York Times (NYT) in terms of valuation.

A look at their profitability metrics deepens the bearish narrative surrounding Newsmax. For instance, Newsmax’s EBIT and EBITDA margins sit at -40.8% and -37.2%, respectively, compared to Fox’s healthy 18.8% and 21.2%. Also, on the bottom line, Newsmax’s net income margin is at -42.2%, in sharp contrast with Fox’s 11.6%.

We spot a similar trend in the liquidity numbers, where Newsmax looks remarkably frail compared to the fortress-like balance sheet of Fox Corp. Fox holds a sizeable $4.8 billion in its cash till, against $7.2 billion in debt, boasting a relatively solid current ratio of 2.45. On the flip side, Newsmax sits at just $82 million in cash, with a current ratio below 1.0 (at 0.96) and quick ratio under 1.0 (at 0.90).

That situation seems unlikely to improve anytime soon, and despite having all that IPO money, it needs its business to fire, given the high cost of producing news programming. Unless it scales up dramatically, expect more dilution ahead and additions to its debt load.

Moreover, its reliance on advertising makes it remarkably vulnerable to changes in the economy and the ad market. Roughly two-thirds of its sales come from advertising, leaving it exposed to ad spending pullbacks or potential boycotts.

Also, as a niche player, far from being ‘brand safe’, Newsmax can't command the same premium CPMS like its peers. On top of that, the elections are in the near-view mirror, so the advertising headwind will become even more pronounced.

Moreover, the snapshot shows the options market is growing sour over Newsmax stock as well. Implied volatility, for instance, sits at an eye-watering 141%, while an IV rank of just 8% suggests that the choppiness could spike even further.

On the volume front, its put-to-call ratio continues hovering above 1.0, peaking at 1.7, with selling pressure outweighing buying pressure. That said, with the durability in put builds and an absence of a major catalyst, the outlook for Newsmax looks mostly bleak.

Recent Developments

Its legal overhang is perhaps the most concerning element for investors to consider with Newsmax. It has already reserved a sizeable $40 million for the Smartmatic settlement, but perhaps the larger Dominion lawsuit is what’s considerably more daunting.

Dominion had initially sought $1.6 billion in damages, but from industry precedent (Fox Corp’s case in particular), the amount is likely to be a lot lower. However, even with a relatively smaller sum, Newsmax’s cash till will likely take a beating.

Also, a Delaware judge has already ruled in favor of Dominion Voting, compounding Newsmax’s woes further. Adding to these risks is Newsmax’s corporate structure, where material hitches in its financial controls have been identified, undermining investor confidence in the process.

On a more positive note, though, Newsmax posted some encouraging viewership numbers of late. Nielsen’s data for April showed a 50% increase in viewership in Q1, a welcome development given its current state.

Perhaps a big part of that is its settlement with DirecTV, restoring access to more than 13 million satellite households. Also, it has inked new partnerships with Armenia and Israel, bringing its channels to expatriate audiences.

Takeaway On Newsmax Stock

All in all, Newsmax stock remains a sell. Its meme-driven surge has fizzled out, and investors are contending with the crushing reality of a cash-burning, unprofitable media outfit. Meanwhile, it's trading comfortably above Fox Corp. and many of its peers in the media space, offering a thin margin for disappointment. On top of that, its legal overhang from Dominion’s defamation suits threatens its business even further, making it a speculative trap that’s best avoided.

data.title: JAAA: Job Done! Price And Dividend Have Recovered
data.description:
I have written a couple of times about Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (NYSEARCA:JAAA), a collateralised Loan Obligation ETF which provides a high level of income with low risk. In my first article I explain what CLOs are, and introduce JAAA and its younger brother, JBBB, explaining the respective risks and returns. I recommend that readers unfamiliar with CLOs refer to that article.

As the first article was written before the ‘Liberation Day’ market downturn, I checked in again with JAAA to see how the fund had lived up to its promise of low-risk returns.

I was pleased to see that there were quite a few comments made by readers, which added to my own understanding of CLO’s and also highlighted areas that investors were concerned about.

As the market develops, we get to see the evidence of how JAAA performs in the areas that investors are concerned about.

The Value Proposition

What does JAAA offer investors?

In a nutshell, JAAA offers a high spread above risk-free rates, while offering lower risk than other investments that have equivalent yields.

This is made possible by the mechanics of CLOs. JAAA invests in the private credit market through pools of private credit which are then collateralised and structured into a range of tranches. The equity tranches at the bottom bear the highest risk and the highest returns. The superior tranches gradually embed more layers of protection, making the default probabilities much lower, and as such attract less yield.

If you turn the structure on its head, you can think of it like the owners of the AAA tranches buying themselves insurance from the lower tranches, and they get to keep what they don’t pay out for that insurance.

Each CLO has its own unique risk profile, and the individual tranches are assessed by independent rating agencies, such as A.M. Best and assigned a credit rating.

Why CLOs Have Better Risk Return Than Other Investments

This is always an important question to ask when an investment pays a high yield than the riskiness seems to merit.

You have to look at who invests in CLOs. Typically, these are banks, insurance companies, hedge funds and BDCs.

So the CLO world generally has institutional investors involved.

This is a speculation on my part, so feel free to shoot me down, but I think that this investor profile pays a premium for the CLO product due to the high liquidity. I also think that the mainly institutional investor base creates a market inefficiency. Fundamentally, retail investors should have lower sensitivity to liquidity, and this should drive down the CLO spreads, however complexity fear and a relatively limited retail visibility prevent this.

Investor Concerns

Why Did JAAA Price Drop When Stocks Sold Off?

Many CLO investors, such as banks and insurance companies, are heavily exposed to liquidity risk. CLO's are liquid, but higher yielding cash proxies, and as such, when there is a market event, this is where quick liquidity can be found. You can raise cash without having to realise large capital losses on beaten down investments.

So even though many market events themselves have little impact on the probability of defaults for AAA CLOs, even those tranches experience liquidity-driven price dips.

This creates a shallow correlation with the major indices. Consider the price movement over the recent months.

As S&P 500 and Nasdaq dropped through March, JBBB dropped in sympathy, while JAAA sailed serenely along. It was only when the markets took a big drop and liquidity dried up in early April that JAAA was impacted.

Several commentators to my articles were concerned about the sell-off in JAAA, which viewed as a proxy for cash. May argument was that there was no fundamental rationale for a drop here, and that we should see mean reversion.

Roll forward just a few weeks, and, with increasing liquidity, JAAA price has recovered.

Why Is The Yield Dropping So Much?

At the beginning of the year, JAAA monthly dividend dropped, from $.26 in Jan to $.21 in March. This seemed hard to explain and raised concerns. Gradually the distributions increased and now is back at $.2456.

JAAA dividends are funded from NII and capital gains on trading in and out of positions. The big drivers of NII are benchmark rates and spreads. CLO tranches will be priced at a spread over a risk-free rate, such as the Fed funds rate. As we know, the Fed cut three times into the end of 2024. However, these cuts don't flow immediately through into JAAA distributions.

So what else is going on?

This one becomes harder to follow, and Janus Henderson doesn't help out much.

I think that the answer is that while we think of JAAA as a single investment, in reality it is a number of long-term investments which operate in a liquid market and are actively managed. Portfolio turnover is 51%

  • CLOs come to maturity and roll over.
  • New CLOs come to the market.
  • Portfolio managers trade in and out of positions.
  • As risk-free rates come down, CLOs trade at lower yields.
  • As liquidity dries up, CLOs trade at higher spreads.
  • Cash increases and earns lower rates until redeployed.

Here I think we have to give the team at Janus Henderson some leeway. They have clearly been managing the portfolio and seeking to balance risk and returns through a dynamic risk environment. Portfolio actions do not have immediate effect, but with the yield recovering, there is clearly an impact.

The important metric to consider is the spread over risk-free. This has remained strong, and as interest rates have come down, there has been a lag in this flowing through to JAAA dividends. It's hard to predict, but I see several scenarios in which the distribution could continue to increase into the next rate cut.

Conclusion

All in all, I think that JAAA investors can be pretty satisfied. Both the share price and the dividend have recovered. JAAA has demonstrated its value in a major market event once again. It continues to pay a 'too good to be true' risk-adjusted return.

I for one, am keeping long JAAA, cheerfully dripping and adding on dips.

Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Refer to www.blackrock.com for current month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 5.25% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 7.08% (Institutional shares) and 7.03% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • Positioning in the mining and energy sectors benefited relative returns, notably overweight holdings in gold companies as gold prices increased. An underweight allocation to the agriculture sector hurt relative performance.
  • At quarter-end, the fund had an overweight position in the mining (36.9%) sector, a neutral position in the energy sector (33.3%), an underweight holding in the agriculture (28.1%) sector, and 1.6% was held in cash and cash equivalents.

Contributors

Detractors

At the sector level, allocations to the mining and energy sectors contributed to relative performance. At the stock level, the fund’s strategic and large overweight positions in gold companies, including Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), AngloGold Ashanti (AU), and Kinross Gold (KGC), positively impacted relative returns as gold prices rose. The holding in DS Smith was helpful as its merger with International Paper (IP) boosted investor sentiment. An off-benchmark position in Heidelberg Materials (OTCPK:HDLMY)(OTCPK:HLBZF) was additive as the company continued to deliver strong financial results and maintained a solid market position.

An underweight exposure to the agriculture sector detracted during the quarter. Overweight positions in diversified mining companies Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF)(OTCPK:GLNCY) and Anglo American (OTCQX:AAUKF) hampered relative returns, with the stocks falling due to market uncertainty about the potential of U.S. tariffs to disrupt economic growth and commodity demand. Packaging companies, including Smurfit WestRock (SW)(OTCPK:SMFTF)(OTCPK:SMFKY), International Paper, and Packaging Corp of America (PKG), faced challenges, which resulted in weaker performance. These companies experienced reduced demand due to a slowdown in e-commerce growth and squeezed margins stemming from increased raw material prices.

Further insight

We view natural resource equities as an effective diversifier in portfolios in the current environment, given persistent inflation, geopolitical tensions, and economic uncertainty. Within the energy sector, we continue to see potential for a wider range of outcomes and opportunities in 2025, due to strong U.S. power demand that has been driven by a reshoring of manufacturing and the development of artificial intelligence data centers. In the mining sector, we expect performance to be driven by the Chinese government’s stimulus measures in the near term and by increased global infrastructure development in the long term.

Investment approach

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Stock and bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. International investing involves special risks including, but not limited to political risks, currency fluctuations, illiquidity and volatility. These risks may be heightened for investments in emerging markets. Investments in natural resources industries can be affected by variations in commodities markets, weather, disease, embargoes, political and economic developments, taxes and other government regulations. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 The S&P Global Natural Resources Index includes 90 of the largest publicly-traded companies in natural resources and commodities businesses that meet specific investability requirements, offering investors diversified and investable equity exposure to agribusiness, energy, and metals & mining.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Medicare Probe Hits UnitedHealth Shares
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

UnitedHealth (UNH) probed by DoJ for potential Medicare fraud; shares slide - report. (00:22) Dick's Sporting Goods close to deal for Foot Locker - WSJ. (01:16) Warren Buffett reveals why he chose to hand over Berkshire's reins - report. (01:59)

This is an abridged transcript.

UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) is under investigation by the Department of Justice for potential Medicare fraud.

The Wall Street Journal reported that the probe is centered around the Medicare Advantage business.

UnitedHealth shares were down ~8% in after-hours trading Wednesday evening. Premarket the UNH is still under pressure, down 5%.

The healthcare-fraud unit at DoJ is spearheading the investigation. The newspaper noted that the division investigates transgressions such as kickbacks that lead to higher Medicare and Medicare payments.

The probe is the latest headache for the health insurance giant, which just had an unexpected CEO change on Tuesday.

Earlier this month, a group of shareholders sued the company alleging it hid the business impact from the backlash surrounding the murder of UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson.

Shares of Foot Locker (NYSE:FL) are up more than 68% in premarket action after reports circulated that the company is in acquisition talks with Dick's Sporting Goods (DKS).

According to the Wall Street Journal, Dick's (DKS) is offering $24 per share, or $2.3B for Foot Locker (NYSE:FL). A deal could be finalized as soon as today.

Foot Locker shares have hovered near record lows around $11, pressured by tariff announcements and Nike’s (NKE) heightened promotional activity to clear outdated inventory.

In another article on Seeking Alpha we’re discussing “Why Dick's Sporting Goods may be getting a good deal if it buys Foot Locker at a premium.” I’ll leave a link to the article in show notes.

Warren Buffett made the decision to step down as Berkshire Hathaway's (NYSE:BRK.B) CEO after finally feeling his age.

The 94-year-old billionaire said in an interview with The Wall Street Journal, "I didn't really start getting old, for some strange reason, until I was about 90," he said. "But when you start getting old, it's irreversible."

Buffett, one of the world's most successful investors, announced earlier this month that he'd step down as CEO in December and hand over the reins to Greg Abel. Buffett will stay on as chairman.

The decision stunned the company and the investing world… not even Abel expected it that day. Many thought Buffett would remain Berkshire's (NYSE:BRK.B) CEO for life.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Trump asks Apple to stop moving iPhone manufacturing to India - report

The Monopoly ETF, a new SEC filing that targets market titans

China lifts curbs on export of rare earth minerals to the U.S.

Catalyst watch:

  • Walmart (WMT) will hold its earnings call at 8am. The retail giant can sway the entire retail sector with its forecast. Costco (COST), Pricesmart (PSMT), and Five Below (FIVE) are the stocks that correlate the tightest with Walmart (WMT) on earnings day. Major suppliers to Walmart (WMT) with the potential to see share price reactions include Fabrinet (FN), Kontoor Brands (KTB), and Cal-Maine Foods (CALM).

  • Take-Two Interactive (TTWO) will hold its earnings conference call at 4:30 pm. The delay of the launch of Grand Theft Auto VI will be a major topic.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is down 4% at $60/barrel. Bitcoin is down 1.7% at $101,000. Gold is down 0.5% at $3,168.

The FTSE 100 is flat and the DAX is down 0.2%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Luminar Technologies (NASDAQ:LAZR) -10% — Shares sank after the company reported mixed Q1 results and announced a leadership shake-up.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Jobless Claims

  • 8:30 am Retail Sales

  • 8:40 am Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell will give a speech at the Thomas Laubach Research Conference concerning Framework Review. The conference is considered a significant event in the Federal Reserve’s ongoing review of its monetary policy strategy, tools, and communication practices.

  • 10:00 am Business Inventories

  • 10:30 am EIA Natural Gas Report

data.title: How Is The U.S. Retail Industry Doing?
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

A bellwether reports

A better picture of the U.S. consumer, along with the retail industry at large, will come into focus today as Walmart (WMT) releases its latest earnings report before the market opens. It's been quite a ride over the past few months due to the uncertainty from the Trump administration's tariffs, which were raised to exorbitant levels before being pulled back for trade talks. A third of what Walmart sells is brought in from outside the U.S., with China and Mexico noted as the "most significant" import markets.

By the numbers: Analysts expect Walmart to disclose revenue of $164.5B and earnings per share of $0.58, which falls into the company's Q1 guidance of $0.57 to $0.58 EPS. U.S. comparable store sales are seen rising 3.6% amid the general trend of consumers stocking up on household goods due to prior fears of higher tariff-related prices. Guidance is also expected to be broad enough to encompass a wide range of outcomes. Walmart previously guided for full-year net sales growth of 3% to 4% and adjusted operating income growth of 3.5% to 5.5%, even as the company integrates its VIZIO acquisition and navigates a less favorable category mix.

Investors will be watching for updates on Walmart's ability to sustain momentum amid ongoing macroeconomic challenges. Other key areas of focus on the conference call will include Walmart's advertising business, e-commerce performance, inventory management, automation, and supply chain and sourcing strategies. Walmart's report is seen as holding more weight than normal, as analysts dive into the implications of growing market share by holding back on price increases vs. protecting margins.

Outlook: Options trading implies a 6% move in Walmart's (WMT) share price after the earnings report is released, which is more implied volatility than usual. Costco (COST), PriceSmart (PSMT), and Five Below (FIVE) are the stocks that closely correlate with Walmart on earnings day, as well as major suppliers like Fabrinet (FN), Kontoor Brands (KTB), and Cal-Maine Foods (CALM), which could additionally see share price reactions. As of Wednesday's close, WMT shares are up about 7% YTD, compared to the flat performance of the S&P 500 (SP500).

What else is happening...

Warren Buffett reveals why he handed over Berkshire's reins.

UnitedHealth faces DOJ probe; Is a turnaround possible?

Dick's (DKS) may get a good deal for Foot Locker (FL).

Monopoly ETF - a new SEC filing that targets market titans.

Apple (AAPL) supplier Foxconn cleared to build fab in India.

U.S. renewable risks? Rogue communication devices found.

Boeing's (BA) $96B deal with Qatar Airways caps strong week.

EU threatens fresh Russia sanctions; Putin won't attend latest talks.

Here's how many subs Netflix now has for ad-supported service.

eToro (ETOR) shares see double-digit gains in market debut.

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

UnitedHealth Group Is In Chaos

SPY: The Correction May Come Soon

Strategically Building A $500,000 Dividend Portfolio: Boosting SCHD's Income With May's High-Yield Picks

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan -1%. Hong Kong -0.8%. China -0.7%. India +1.5%.
In Europe, at midday, London +0.1%. Paris -0.2%. Frankfurt -0.1%.
Futures at 6:00, Dow -0.3%. S&P -0.5%. Nasdaq -0.7%. Crude -4% to $60.60. Gold -0.6% to $3,170.70. Bitcoin -1.6% to $101,832.
Ten-year Treasury Yield -2 bps to 4.51%.

On The Calendar

Companies reporting today include Alibaba (BABA) and Applied Materials (AMAT).

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: International General Insurance: Soft Q1, Still A Strong Business
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I continue to view International General Insurance (IGIC) as an undervalued specialty [re]insurer with a strong track record of underwriting discipline and shareholder value creation. I previously covered IGIC on April 12th, 2025. Readers can refer to that article for a detailed background; here I update my view after the 1Q 2025 earnings results.

IGIC's worldwide specialty portfolio spans long-tail, short-tail and treaty reinsurance lines. In 2024, the company achieved a 79.9% combined ratio, record net income and grew its book value per share by nearly 20%. Management emphasizes that IGIC has a long history of generating value through underwriting discipline, intelligent risk selection and shifting to those lines and markets with the best risk-adjusted returns. I believe those strengths are still intact: IGIC's diverse global footprint and conservative reserving mean it can navigate volatility better than many peers, and its returns exceed capital via dividends and buybacks of ~$50 Mn in 2024. Against this backdrop, IGIC trades at a low P/E multiple at 8.6x, implying a significant upside when normalized. Even though its 1Q 2025 earnings were softer, I see this quarter as an anomaly in a still-strong business and I remain bullish on IGIC's long-term outlook.

Business Overview

IGIC is an international specialist [re]insurance group with deep technical expertise across a highly diversified portfolio. The company underwrites specialty risks worldwide, including energy, property, aviation, political violence, marine, financial institutions and treaty reinsurance. This is done through three segments, namely: Long-tail, Specialty short tail and reinsurance. This broad line and geographic diversification is central to IGIC's strategy. The company emphasizes an underwriting first culture focused on profitable growth and lower volatility. IGIC has a strong balance sheet and an A-rated balance sheet by S&P rating, reflecting its prudent reserving and conservative investment approach. Management and founders (The Jabsheh family) collectively own a large stake (~35%) in the business, aligning interests with long-term value creation. It is important to mention that IGIC has consistently returned capital to shareholders. For example, it repurchased 159k shares in 1Q 2025 and paid over $39.7 Mn in dividends, including a special dividend of $0.85.

1Q 2025 Earnings Review

IGIC's 1Q 2025 results showed top-line growth but underwriting headwinds. Gross written premiums [GWP] grew 13.7% y/y to $206.5Mn and this was mainly driven by treaty reinsurance. The net premiums earned were flat at $113 Mn. Underwriting income fell to $27.9 Mn as the company incurred elevated losses. This led to a combined ratio of 94.4% compared to 74.1%. This was primarily due to heavy, catastrophic and large-loss activity caused by the California Wildfires.

Despite the weak underwriting quarter, management characterized the results as "relatively solid" given the circumstances, noting that the results

Clearly underscore our value proposition and the strength of our diversification strategy...

significantly elevated natural catastrophe and large loss activity and significant macroeconomic uncertainty...

Waleed Jabsheh, CEO

IGIC also highlighted that it returned $43.5 Mn to shareholders in 1Q 2025 and this underscores the company's firm capital return policy amid earnings pressure. In short, the 1Q 2025 earnings release stresses that this quarter was an outlier in terms of losses and IGIC remains optimistic about 2025.

We remain positive about the year ahead.

Waleed Jabsheh, CEO

Growth and Profitability

Over the long term, IGIC's growth and profitability fundamentals remain attractive. The company has compounded GWP at about 11% since 2015, expanding especially in reinsurance and growing its specialty short-tail lines. This growth has been profitable thanks to IGIC's disciplined underwriting. In 2024, the combined ratio was an industry-leading 79.9%, meaning the company earned nearly 20% of premiums as underwriting profit. IGIC's net income margins of ~25% are above P&C median and its long-term ROE has been strong at ~22.6%. The company's 10-year average combined ratio is around 88%, which is typically 3 to 4 points above peer insurers.

The recent quarter's spike in losses should moderate. IGIC's geographic and product diversification helps offset shocks. For example, losses were concentrated in energy and reinsurance lines, while other segments performed better. In contrast, peers like MCY were hit harder by CAT losses. MCY's 1Q 2025 combined ratio jumped to 119.2% due to the LA wildfires. IGIC, by diversifying across 200+ countries and significant reinsurance, managed to stay profitable on a 94% combined ratio. Looking ahead, IGIC plans steady expansion. With a strong A-rated balance sheet and no debt, IGIC can even step up selective underwriting in 2025 if conditions improve.

Valuation

On a valuation basis, IGIC still looks cheap relative to its intrinsic and peer norms. IGIC trades at ~8.6x TTM P/E and ~1.6x Book. The earnings multiple is actually below the specialty insurance sector median of 10.6x, even as IGIC has higher ROEs. IGIC's P/B is above the sector median of 1.2x, but this reflects its sustained high ROE.

If we compare IGIC with Mercury General (MCY) which faced crippling loss this quarter, it trades at about 14x earnings, reflecting a different risk profile. In my view, IGIC should justify a higher multiple as market cycles turn. For instance, if IGIC [EPS] approaches ~3.00 as guidance suggests, and we assign a 12x P/E, the stock would be $36. Even a more conservative 10x P/E yields a $30 share price. This 50% upside target factors in continued growth, disciplined expense control and ongoing capital returns. Analysts often value insurers by book or embedded value. On IGIC's 2024 tangible book of $14.65, a 2x book value would imply a share price of $29.3. Considering IGIC's 20% book value growth last year, a 2x book multiple would imply a $29.3 stock price if execution stays on track. If we average the two methods, we arrive at a blended target price of $33, implying a 44% upside.

IGIC's Key Risk

While I remain bullish on IGIC, it is important to acknowledge some of the key risks that might hinder our target price from being achieved. The biggest risk I see for IGIC is CAT losses and Large-loss exposures. As 1Q 2025 showed, extreme natural disasters or large casualty events can spike losses and hurt earnings. A series of bad quarters could pressure the stock further. Secondly, there is the risk of underwriting cycles. Soft pricing markets or overaggressive expansion in unprofitable lines could degrade IGIC's underwriting edge and therefore, management must stick to strict, disciplined risk selection. Lastly, if earnings stay weak, future dividends and buybacks could be smaller, limiting shareholder yield.

I view these risks as manageable given IGIC's track record. The company has navigated past cycles, as Jabsheh notes

Consistently demonstrated our ability to manage through all market cycles...

Final Thoughts

In summary, I believe IGIC's 1Q 2025 softness is temporary in the context of a high-quality insurance franchise. The fundamentals remain solid, disciplined underwriting, diversified operations, a strong A-rated balance sheet and shareholder-aligned capital deployment. Management's commentary underlines confidence, "we remain positive about the year ahead" and emphasizes that IGIC's diversified portfolio helps mitigate today's challenges. With IGIC trading at a low single-digit earnings multiple, I see the stock as offering asymmetric upside. My thesis that IGIC will compound book value and earnings via prudent underwriting and capital returns still holds. I expect combined ratios and ROEs to revert closer to historical levels in the coming quarters, which should drive share price appreciation. On that basis, I rate IGIC a Buy with a price target of $33 in the next 12–24 months, reflecting an expanded P/E relative to today.

data.title: Atkore: I See More Runway Ahead
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Back on April 7th, I published an article on Atkore Inc. (ATKR) and initiated a Buy rating because the stock was trading at an FWD PEG of just 0.88x, a 40% discount to its peers. This is while structural tailwinds in US infrastructure, electrification and hyperscale data center build-outs were set to lift volumes and margins. I also argued that management's focus on reversing margin compression and reducing leverage would unlock meaningful equity upside. Fast-forward to the 2Q 2025 release and the core pillars of that thesis remain firmly intact, even after the non-cash HDPE impairment. Organic volume grew 5%, productivity initiatives expanded sequential EBITDA margins to 16.6% and management maintained a full-year guidance for $400 Mn of adjusted EBITDA despite pricing headwinds. Also, the newly reinstated 25% tariffs on imported steel conduit tilt the competitive field further in Atkore's favor, underscoring my original view that the company's domestic manufacturing footprint is a long-term moat.

Valuation is still compelling at ~8x EV/EBITDA compared to the sector median of 11x. The market assigns no premium for those tariff benefits. There is also the share repurchase program and the accelerated demand coming from grid hardening, AI-driven data centers and water infrastructure upgrades. With the catalysts, I highlighted in April now visible in quarterly numbers and management's capital return strategy adding another dimension to total shareholder yield, I reiterate my Buy rating and see fair value of $78 in the next 12 months.

Business Overview

Atkore is a leading US manufacturer of electrical conduit, cable management and safety infrastructure products with a largely domestic supply chain. In its 2Q 2025 earnings report, management highlighted its resilient, diversified portfolio and US sourcing as key advantages in a tough market,

Our Preliminary estimated second quarter results underscore the resilience of our portfolio and the dedication our team with most products sources and manufactured in the US.

Bill Waltz, CEO

Atkore sells electrical raceway and safety infrastructure products into construction and industrial markets. Its two reporting segments are electrical Raceway which includes steel conduits, fittings and cable trays. The second is Safety Infrastructure which includes safety products, fiber conduits and construction services. In 2Q, about 70% of sales were in the electrical segment and accounted for about $492.7 Mn, while Safety Infra (S&I) accounted for $209.3 Mn. Segment profitability shifted sharply. Electrical EBITDA fell 53% while S&I jumped 41%. It is important to mention that Atkore's business model emphasizes on US production and heavily on domestic production and, as noted by the CEO;

Most of what we make and sell originates with materials, labor and equipment in the same geography...

...We are more optimistic about demand for US-made steel conduit in 2025.

Bill Waltz, CEO

That manufacturing footprint dovetails almost perfectly with the "Buy America" and re-industrialization themes championed by President Trump's administration. By keeping production onshore, ATKR shortens supply chains, mitigates freight volatility and positions itself as a dependable partner for federally funded infrastructure and data center projects that require US content. Over the long term, I believe this alignment should translate into steadier volume growth as public-sector spending stipulates domestic sourcing and superior pricing power versus import-reliant rivals. Lastly, a strategic moat should future administrations to continue or expand the Buy America mandate. In short, Atkore's US manufacturing bias is no longer just a cost advantage, but it is becoming a regulatory and political moat that supports my bullish outlook.

Growth and Profitability Catalysts

When I initiated coverage in April, I expected a mid-single-digit organic growth and incremental margin lift to drive multiple expansion. 1Q 2025 numbers confirm that trajectory. My core growth catalysts are already visible in the run-rate.

First and foremost, organic shipments rose roughly 5% y/y in 2Q 2025, snapping back from the 5% decline in 1Q 2025. That rebound was powered by metal framing, cable management and construction services orders tied to hyperscale data center builds and industrial reshoring. With a healthy backlog and additional framing capacity coming in sight, I expect a mid-single-digit volume gain through 2025.

Secondly, most of the y/y revenue drag came from PVC and steel conduit price normalization. This means that sequentially, steel conduit pricing is already firmed and the newly introduced 25% Section 232 tariff effectively places a floor under import competition. It is important that the recently announced tariff pause between US-China does not touch on the Section 232 duties. As those duties filter through distributor inventories, I see modest price tailwinds returning to 4Q 2025.

Thirdly, the company's lean initiatives and freshly ratified 5-year labor contract pushed 2Q 2025 adjusted EBITDA margin up by 160bps to 16.6%. This is despite lower ASPs. I expect another 100bps of efficiency gains over the next quarter as the new Atkore business system projects mature.

Valuation

Atkore trades at historically low multiples despite high single-digit growth rates. The company's market cap plus net debt yields an EV of $3 Bn. Using the midpoint of FY2025 guidance, i.e. adjusted EBITDA of $400Mn and an ~8x EV/EBITDA multiple, yields an EV of $3.2 Bn. This represents an equity of $2.6 Bn and if the number is divided by the shares outstanding of ~33.65 Mn, I arrive at a target price of $78.

By comparison, many peers and also the general sector median trades at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 11x. For instance, Hubbell (HUBB) trades at EV/EBITDA of 14x, suggesting that ATKR is discounted in comparison to its peers. If you look at the figure below, ATKR is trading at a 34% discount relative to its peers. When the sector median is 11.33x, Atkore trades at a FWD EV/EBITDA multiple of ~8x.

Again, if you look at the FWD P/E (Non-GAAP), Atkore is trading at 10.89x. This is using the FY2025 [EPS] guidance of $6.30. This means that PEG is below the sector median given its growth rates, which is clearly evident in the figure above. While ATKR trades a FWD PEG of 1.09x, the sector median stands at 1.73x, suggesting a 37% reduction. Most consensus Wall Street analysts target only an average of $74, implying a limited upside. However, my analysis, considering Atkore's growth outlook, debt adjusted capital returns and likely cyclic recovery in infrastructure, supports a bullish valuation with a 12-month price target of $78.

Atkore's key risks

The biggest risk I see for Atkore is that margin pressure. The sharp y/y drop in PVC and steel-conduit pricing reminded me how quickly gross profit can erode when commodity curves roll over or import competition intensifies. While I believe the worst of the reset is behind us, and now Section 232 tariffs now serve as a floor, any prolonged softness in hot-rolled coil or a resurgence of discount-priced imports could hurt margins.

Secondly, macro and policy volatility remain a wild card. My longer-term view leans on sustained federal and private sector investment in data centers, manufacturing reshoring and grid upgrades. These trends are currently aligning with the current political climate. However, a change in administration, a deeper construction slowdown or an unexpected shock to industrial activity could sap volume growth and undermine the secular narrative

Final Thoughts

After revisiting 2Q 2025 results, the tariff backdrop and my original valuation work, I still see ATKR as an undervalued company. Atkore is a US-centric infrastructure play with accelerating volume and improving efficiency. At ~8x FWD EV/EBITDA the stock offers a compelling upside to my $78 price target, so I'm sticking with my Buy call.

data.title: High Yield And Trade: Why Asia Could Be A Compelling Source Of Stability
data.description:
Executive Summary

  • High yield investors search for high income and are willing to take on risks as a result. However, with potential tariffs on imports to the US on the horizon, the cash flows of US high yield issuers that are dependent on imports could be severely impacted.
  • By contrast, the issuers in the KraneShares Asia Pacific High Income USD Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:KHYB), which is actively managed by Nikko Asset Management, have no revenue exposure to the United States, insulating them from direct tariff risks. Nikko Asset Management is a Japanese multinational investment company and one of the largest asset managers in the world, with over $235 billion in assets under management (AUM) as of December 2024.4
  • KHYB has a 12-month trailing distribution rate of 10.32%, higher than all US-listed emerging markets bond ETFs, and a 30-Day SEC yield of 7.54% as of May 13, 2025, and all bonds held in the portfolio are denominated in USD, resulting in no direct currency risk.3
  • The number of bonds trading below 80% of par value, in the United States surged year-over-year in May, and are significantly above the current levels in Asia, which means US high yield could see more defaults this year and reflecting the high risk premium placed on US companies reliant on imports.
  • Nikko believes Asian economies are relatively well-positioned to weather a potential global economic downturn and increased trade protectionism in the US and Europe due to continued easing of monetary and fiscal policies from central banks, low inflation, and large and growing regional markets.
  • We believe KHYB can complement any global fixed income allocation due to its relatively high yield, low average duration, and low correlation to global fixed income markets.2

Most Asian nations run trade surpluses with the United States. As such, many were targeted by US President Trump’s “Liberation Day” reciprocal tariffs. Many Southeast Asian countries and China face potentially over 30% reciprocal tariffs. Within Asia, China, the Philippines, India, and Malaysia are likely to be more insulated as domestic demand comprises between 60% and 70% of gross domestic product (GDP). China responded with significant tariffs on all US imports, which led the US to escalate the situation, raising the tariff rate on China-made goods to 145%.

Meanwhile, Vietnam’s economy is highly exposed to US exports, contributing over 20% of its GDP, and was hit with a 46% tariff. Vietnam has already offered to remove all tariffs on US imports, but it remains to be seen how the US might respond.

In a recent report titled Trade Policy Impacts On Asian Fixed Income, Nikko stated that technology hardware issuers and consumer goods-focused industrial companies, including auto and electric vehicle (EV) batteries, with sizable revenue exposure from the United States, could be directly affected by tariffs. However, they noted that these issuers are nearly all investment grade, leading to their more favorable view of high yield in the current environment.

Asian high yield bonds are dominated by issuers focusing on domestic and regional business with limited direct exposure to the United States, and as of March 31, 2025, financials, real estate, utilities, and Macau casino operators accounted for over 70% of the JPMorgan Asia Credit Index (JACI) Non-Investment Grade, KHYB's benchmark. These sectors derive a limited revenue contribution from the US. Based on bottom-up screening, issuers in the high yield index whose businesses are likely affected by the reciprocal tariff (i.e., more than 20% of revenue comes from the US market) are a handful of healthcare and supply chain companies, representing only 1% of the index. KHYB, which is actively managed by Nikko Asset Management, a leader in Asia fixed income with over 30 years of experience,4 does not currently have a position in any of these companies.

The top 5 issuers within KHYB are Ping An Real Estate (4.3%), Wynn Macau (3.8%), Greentown China Holdings (3.6%), Standard Chartered (3.6%), Vedanta Resources Finance (3.3%).2 None of these companies export goods to the US. Therefore, we expect a limited fundamental impact from the announced reciprocal tariffs. However, the indirect impact will likely hit most sectors and issuers, with the heightened risk of a global recession depending on how long the trade conflict lasts. Banks will have profitability impacted by lower interest rates and higher credit costs. Meanwhile, commodities-related credits will likely be impacted by lower commodity prices.

Going forward, Nikko's base case is for most economies to negotiate with the US to lower tariffs and thus mitigate some of the impact from the initial announcement. However, significant uncertainty surrounds this, and there is a risk of increased tit-for-tat measures in the near term. Partially mitigating these risks, most Asian economies entered this period of higher volatility with relatively robust external, fiscal, and domestic demand conditions, which should provide a good buffer to withstand the challenges ahead, according to Nikko. Notably, China’s policymakers have already begun discussing stimulus measures. In addition, most regional central banks retain room to ease monetary policy to support domestic demand, and inflation has declined in the region. Also, thanks to easy access to fast-growing regional markets, Asian high yield bonds are less exposed to trade with the US than other Emerging Market bonds.

Nikko expects Asian central banks to continue their current path of rate cuts to mitigate growth risks. They see domestic funding for Asian issuers to remain favorable and Asia high-yield companies to benefit from lower interest expenses domestically. In addition, many Asia high yield companies have tapped the offshore dollar market for refinancing and extended their maturity ladder in the past year. Nikko expects most high yield issuers will not face imminent repayment risk, supporting continued lower default rates in the space.

However, the situation is still playing out in performance. The announcement of the reciprocal tariffs on April 2nd led to an initial selloff in Asian high yield bonds. Nikko believes that their strong balance sheets and relatively low exposure to US trade become apparent, Asian bonds may re-rate to the upside. Bonds trading below 80% of par value, in the United States surged year-over-year in May, and are significantly above the current levels in Asia, which means US high yield could see more defaults this year.

KHYB, an access vehicle for Asia's high yield bond market, offers an average duration of 2 years and a correlation of only 0.3 to US high yield over the past three years.

KHYB, an access vehicle for Asia's high yield bond market, offers an average duration of 2 years and a correlation of only 0.3 to US high yield over the past three years.

Conclusion

Unlike investment grade issuers, high yield issuers mainly focus on domestic markets with limited US revenue exposure. As such, the holdings in the KraneShares Asia Pacific High Income Bond ETF would have a minimal direct impact from tariffs. We believe Asian economies are relatively well-positioned to weather a potential global economic downturn and increased trade protectionism in the US and Europe due to continued easing of monetary and fiscal policies from central banks, low inflation, and large and growing regional markets. This makes Asia’s high yield bond market an attractive option for investors compared to other emerging debt markets. We also believe KHYB can complement any global fixed income allocation due to its relatively high yield, low average duration, and low correlation to global fixed income markets.

Citations:

  1. Data from Bloomberg as of 3/31/2025.
  2. Data from KraneShares as of 3/31/2025. Please click here for underlying data as of the most recent quarter-end. Holdings are subject to change.
  3. Data from Bloomberg and KraneShares as of 5/2/2025. The 12-Month Distribution Rate includes return of capital. For more information on the sources of the Fund’s latest distribution, please refer to the Fund’s 19a Notice.
  4. Data from Nikko Asset Management as of 12/31/2024.

Definitions:

JPMorgan Asia Credit Index Non-Investment Grade: The J.P. Morgan Asia Credit Index Non-Investment Grade Index consists of liquid US-dollar denominated debt instruments issued out of Asia ex-Japan. It is based on the composition and established methodology of the J.P. Morgan Asia Credit Index (JACI), which is market capitalization weighted. The index is limited to issuers classified as non-investment grade based on the middle rating between Moody’s, Fitch, and S&P.

Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Index ("US High Yield"): The Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Bond Index measures the USD-denominated, high yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody's, Fitch and S&P is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below. Bonds from issuers with an emerging markets country of risk, based on Bloomberg’s EM country definition, are excluded.

Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN): ASEAN is a regional grouping of 10 states in Southeast Asia that aims to promote economic and security cooperation among its ten members.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP): The total value of all goods and services produced within a country in a given timeframe, usually one year.

Default Rate: The default rate is the percentage of all outstanding loans that a lender has written off as unpaid after a prolonged period of missed payments.

Investment Grade: The term ‘investment grade’ refers to a company's credit quality. To be considered an investment-grade issue, the company must be rated at 'BBB' or higher by Standard and Poor's or Moody's. Anything below this 'BBB' rating is considered non-investment grade or high yield.

High Yield: The term ‘high yield’ refers to the quality of a company's credit. To be considered a high yield issue, the company must be rated lower than ‘BBB’ by Standard and Poor's or Moody's. Anything 'BBB’ or above is considered investment grade.

30-Day SEC Yield: A standard yield calculation developed by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) that allows for fairer comparisons of bond funds. It is based on the most recent 30-day period covered by the fund's filings with the SEC. Unsubsidized yield does not adjust for any fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements in effect.

12-Month Trailing Distribution Rate: The distribution yield an investor would have received if they had held the fund over the last twelve months, assuming the most recent NAV. The 12-Month Distribution Rate is calculated by summing any income, capital gains and return of capital distributions over the past twelve months and dividing by the sum of the most recent NAV and any capital gain distributions made over the same period. 12-Month Distribution Rate includes return of capital. For the Fund’s fiscal year to date, please refer here for the SEC 19a Notice with a breakdown of each monthly distribution.

Tariff: A levy or tax charged on imports into a country from abroad.
